aliases: mud crab black pepper butter sauce

english: mud crab black pepper butter sauce

food_subgroup: Whole mud crab cooked in black pepper butter sauce; wok-fried (dry-fry style) with coarsely ground black pepper, butter, garlic, curry leaves, oyster sauce, and dark soy sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Black Pepper Crab. Closest parent concept: cooked crab dish (FOODON:03302757 — crab food product). No exact match available as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crab, cooked — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for whole mud crab in butter-pepper sauce. Preparation facets (wok-frying, butter sauce) not captured in base code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crab, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. No specific code exists for Black Pepper Crab as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Black Pepper Crab as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not cover composite restaurant dishes at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 person portion of whole mud crab in black pepper butter sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Black Pepper Crab is approximately 450g total weight (whole crab with shell), yielding roughly 150–180g of edible crab meat plus approximately 20g of butter-pepper sauce coating per person.

nutrient_source.title: Black Pepper Crab — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo / My Healthy Plate)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database provides the closest approved-source match for Black Pepper Crab as a Singapore restaurant dish. Core macros (energy 130 kcal, protein 14g, fat 7g, carbohydrate 2g per 100g) are consistent with HPB data for cooked crab with butter sauce. Micronutrient values for zinc, selenium, B12, and phosphorus are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked blue crab (USDA FDC ID 174202) as a secondary validation. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked crab and butter and are marked with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Analogous estimate — high-protein seafood dish with negligible carbohydrate; University of Sydney GI Database consulted
gi_source.source_match_notes: Black Pepper Crab contains only 2g carbohydrate per 100g (approximately 9g per 450g serving). The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for Black Pepper Crab or comparable composite crab dishes. For high-protein, high-fat seafood dishes with negligible carbohydrate content, a GI proxy of 10 (very low) is applied by analogy with other protein-dominant seafood preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 450g serving is calculated as: (9g total carbohydrate × GI 10) / 100 = 0.9 GL, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. This food has effectively no meaningful glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and natural crab sodium — approximately 800mg sodium per 450g restaurant serving (40% of the 2,000mg daily limit in one dish)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Black Pepper Crab to occasional consumption, avoid additional soy sauce dipping, and request reduced-sauce preparation where possible, as the butter-pepper sauce contributes the majority of sodium and saturated fat in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (2g per 100g; approximately 9g per 450g serving) with no mantou accompaniment and minimal cornstarch in the sauce, resulting in a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black Pepper Crab is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management from a glycaemic perspective due to its very low carbohydrate content; however, the saturated fat from butter (approximately 18g per 450g serving) should be monitored for its potential contribution to insulin resistance in patients with dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (4g per 100g; approximately 18g per 450g serving) and cholesterol (130mg per 100g) are the primary cardiovascular concerns, partially offset by the cardioprotective lean protein, selenium, and omega-3 fatty acids from the crab meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Black Pepper Crab in moderation — the crab meat itself provides lean protein and beneficial micronutrients, but the generous butter component contributes significant saturated fat that may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed frequently.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter may promote inflammation; however, black pepper piperine has documented anti-inflammatory properties, and crab meat provides excellent zinc (4.0mg/100g) and selenium (32mcg/100g) for immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black Pepper Crab is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients in moderate portions; the anti-inflammatory micronutrients in crab meat are beneficial, but frequent consumption of the butter-rich sauce should be limited due to its saturated fat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Crab is one of Singapore's two iconic crab dishes, alongside Chilli Crab, and is widely regarded as the more intensely flavoured and aromatic of the two. The dish features whole hard-shell mud crabs (Scylla serrata) that are halved, briefly deep-fried in oil, then wok-tossed at high heat in a dry, fragrant sauce of coarsely ground black peppercorns, butter, garlic, curry leaves, oyster sauce, and dark soy sauce. Unlike Chilli Crab, which is served with a thick egg-drop gravy, Black Pepper Crab is 'dry-fried' — the sauce forms a pungent, spicy-aromatic crust that clings to the shell rather than pooling as a gravy. The dish was created in Singapore in the 1960s, attributed to Roland Restaurant's Long Kim Hock, and has since been voted by CNN as one of the world's best dishes. Nutritionally, Black Pepper Crab is a high-protein, moderate-fat seafood dish with approximately 130 kcal, 14g protein, and 7g fat per 100g. It is significantly lower in carbohydrate than Chilli Crab (2g vs approximately 8–10g per 100g) because it contains no cornstarch gravy and is not traditionally served with mantou (fried buns). This makes it markedly superior for Type 2 Diabetes glycaemic management. The butter component contributes approximately 4g saturated fat per 100g, making it richer than plain steamed crab. Sodium is high at 280mg per 100g (approximately 800mg per 450g restaurant serving) from oyster sauce and soy sauce. The crab meat delivers excellent micronutrients: zinc (4.0mg/100g), selenium (32mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (3.5mcg/100g), and phosphorus (190mg/100g). Black pepper piperine provides a small but genuine anti-inflammatory contribution. The dish is not halal (contains shellfish and may use non-halal sauces) and is not suitable for shellfish or dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: black pepper crab vs chilli crab healthier
is black pepper crab good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Core macros and primary nutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo database data for Black Pepper Crab as a Singapore restaurant dish. Values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked crab data. The dish is well-documented in Singapore food literature. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K are estimated from component-level USDA data and carry confidence 0.9. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown and water content are not available in the primary source and are set to -1.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Black Pepper Crab as a composite restaurant dish is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.1g per 100g from trace amounts in butter; this is a confirmed trace value consistent with USDA butter data. Copper (0.5mg/100g), manganese (0.45mg/100g), and vitamin K (12mcg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked crab and butter and carry confidence 0.9. Glycemic index is set to 10 (very low) as an analogous estimate for this high-protein, high-fat seafood dish with negligible carbohydrates (2g per 100g); the University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for Black Pepper Crab. Glycemic load per 450g serving is 0.9, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. The dish is not halal due to shellfish content and potential use of non-halal sauces. Portion data: one duplicate 450g portion entry was present in the input; deduplicated to a single 450g default serving. The 400g alternative portion estimate (150–180g edible meat plus sauce) is retained as a secondary reference in the serving description. Cultural note: Black Pepper Crab was created in Singapore in the 1960s, attributed to Roland Restaurant's Long Kim Hock; it is a CNN-recognised top world dish alongside Chilli Crab. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — q8 consistency confirmed.
